world-news.title: Trump says Russia and Ukraine to observe three-day ceasefire
world-news.summary: U.S. President Donald Trump announced that Russia and Ukraine have agreed to a temporary three-day ceasefire, though both countries have accused each other of breaking previous ceasefires. A ceasefire is an agreement to stop fighting temporarily. This matters because the Russia-Ukraine war has killed hundreds of thousands and displaced millions since Russia invaded in 2022. If a ceasefire holds, it could lead to peace negotiations and reduce suffering. However, the announcement comes amid tensions, with both sides claiming the other violated earlier truces during celebrations of Soviet victory over Nazi Germany. A successful pause could be a first step toward ending one of the world's deadliest current conflicts.

world-news.title: Iran accuses US of 'reckless military adventure'
world-news.summary: Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araghchi accused the United States of undermining diplomatic solutions through military action. The ongoing tension stems from decades of hostile relations between Iran and the U.S., dating back to Iran's 1979 revolution. Araghchi claims that whenever Iran and the U.S. approach peace negotiations, America launches attacks instead. This matters globally because Iran is a major Middle Eastern power with nuclear technology, and U.S.-Iran conflict could destabilize the entire region, affecting oil prices and international security. The accusation suggests diplomatic talks are failing, which raises fears of escalating military confrontation that could draw in other nations like Israel and Saudi Arabia.

world-news.title: Hungary's new PM to be sworn in during 'regime change' party
world-news.summary: Péter Magyar, leader of the Tisza party, is being sworn in as Hungary's new Prime Minister after his party's landslide election victory nearly a month ago, ending 16 years of rule by Viktor Orbán. Orbán, who led Hungary since 2010, is known for weakening democratic institutions and aligning closely with Russia, which alarmed the European Union and NATO allies. Magyar's victory represents a major political shift toward democracy and Western alignment. This matters because Hungary is an EU and NATO member, and its direction affects European unity and Western strength against Russian aggression. The "regime change" language reflects how significantly Hungarians rejected Orbán's authoritarian approach, signaling potential reconciliation with democratic European values.

world-news.title: Iran war live: US expects Tehran's reply to peace deal; 'clashes' in Hormuz
world-news.summary: The United States is awaiting Iran's response to a proposed peace deal while military clashes are occurring in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical global shipping passage. Meanwhile, Israel killed 31 people in southern Lebanon, including a rescue worker, according to Lebanon's National News Agency. The Strait of Hormuz is one of the world's most important waterways, carrying roughly one-third of global oil shipments, so any military conflict there threatens worldwide energy prices and economic stability. These simultaneous developments—potential peace talks with Iran and active Israeli operations in Lebanon—represent a dangerous escalation in Middle Eastern tensions. The situation involves multiple countries and could trigger a wider regional war affecting billions of people globally.

world-news.title: Libya's largest oil refinery halts operations during fighting
world-news.summary: Libya's Zawiya refinery, the country's largest oil production facility, has shut down operations as a precautionary measure following explosions and gunfire nearby during ongoing fighting. A refinery is an industrial plant that processes crude oil into usable products like gasoline. This shutdown matters globally because Libya is a major African oil producer, and any disruption affects worldwide oil supplies and prices, which impacts everything from gas at pumps to heating costs. The closure reflects Libya's ongoing instability since its 2011 civil war, with various armed groups competing for power and resources. When oil supplies decrease, prices rise worldwide, affecting economies everywhere and potentially worsening inflation, a measure of how fast prices increase.

world-news.title: Greece examines mystery naval drone found in Ionian Sea
world-news.summary: Greek authorities are investigating an unidentified military drone discovered in the Ionian Sea that reportedly contained explosives. A drone is an unmanned aircraft controlled remotely. Greek media suggests the drone could be Ukrainian or connected to the Russia-Ukraine war. This matters because it indicates military operations are expanding beyond Ukraine's borders into NATO territory, specifically Greek waters. Greece is an EU and NATO member, so discovering foreign military equipment in its waters raises serious security concerns. If the drone is Russian, it signals Russian aggression toward NATO allies; if Ukrainian, it shows the conflict spreading geographically. Either scenario is alarming because it could trigger NATO involvement, potentially expanding the Ukraine war into a much larger European conflict affecting millions.

world-news.title: International cyber attack disrupts swathe of universities and schools
world-news.summary: A hacking group breached Canvas, academic software used by thousands of schools and universities worldwide, causing widespread disruptions to education globally. A cyber attack is when hackers gain unauthorized access to computer systems to steal data or cause damage. This attack matters internationally because it affects students and teachers across multiple continents, disrupting lessons and access to grades and assignments. Educational institutions are critical infrastructure, and targeting them demonstrates how vulnerable global systems are to cyber threats. The incident highlights growing risks from organized hacking groups and suggests similar attacks could target hospitals, power grids, or government systems. This raises questions about cybersecurity investment and international cooperation to prevent attacks that can destabilize essential services millions depend on daily.

world-news.title: Laura Fernandez sworn in as Costa Rica's new president
world-news.summary: Laura Fernandez has been sworn in as Costa Rica's new right-wing president with her party holding an absolute majority in the legislature, the parliament that creates laws. Her right-wing party won control of both the presidency and Congress, giving them significant power to reshape Costa Rica's policies. This matters in Latin American geopolitics because Costa Rica is a democratic leader in the region, and Fernandez's right-wing government may shift policies on economy, environment, and foreign relations. The election reflects broader Latin American trends toward conservative leadership, similar to movements in other countries. Understanding Costa Rica's political direction helps predict regional stability, trade relationships, and environmental policies affecting Central America, which impacts migration patterns and U.S. diplomatic relationships in its hemisphere.

world-news.title: Worldwide race to trace passengers from hantavirus-hit cruise ship
world-news.summary: Health authorities from about 12 countries are tracking passengers from the MV Hondius cruise ship following a hantavirus outbreak, with the UN health agency confirming at least five confirmed cases. Hantavirus is a potentially deadly virus spread through contact with infected rodents or their droppings, causing severe respiratory illness. This matters globally because cruise ships travel internationally, so passengers from one ship could spread disease across continents. The UN (United Nations) health agency involvement shows how serious outbreaks require worldwide coordination. When diseases spread internationally, they become pandemics—worldwide epidemics—that can overwhelm healthcare systems and economies. This situation demonstrates why countries must share health information quickly and why international disease surveillance systems are critical for preventing small outbreaks from becoming catastrophic global health crises affecting millions.

world-news.title: Could Labour and Conservative party dominance in UK politics be ending?
world-news.summary: Britain's Prime Minister faces pressure to resign after his party suffered massive losses in local elections, raising questions about whether Labour and Conservative parties will continue dominating UK politics. Local elections determine who leads cities and councils, and massive losses suggest voters are rejecting traditional parties. This matters globally because Britain is a major economic and military power with permanent UN Security Council influence, so political instability could affect international decision-making. The shift away from two-party dominance mirrors trends worldwide where voters are frustrated with establishment politics and turning to alternative parties. If British politics fragments into multiple parties, it could complicate trade negotiations, military alliances, and diplomatic efforts Britain engages in. The election results signal deeper discontent among British voters that could reshape European and global geopolitics.

science-space.title: Scientists found 10,000 possible exoplanets hiding in NASA data
science-space.summary: Researchers have discovered approximately 10,000 candidate exoplanets (planets orbiting stars outside our solar system) buried in existing NASA telescope data that had never been properly analyzed before. Scientists used advanced computer algorithms and machine learning—artificial intelligence trained to recognize patterns—to sift through massive datasets collected by NASA missions. This is huge because it more than doubles the number of known exoplanet candidates and could revolutionize our understanding of how common planets are in the universe. Each of these 10,000 candidates needs further verification to confirm they're actually planets, but finding them suggests there may be countless worlds out there waiting to be studied. This discovery shows how old data can yield entirely new scientific breakthroughs when examined with modern technology.

science-space.title: Black holes slamming into scorching stars may be causing mysterious blue flashes in the cosmos
science-space.summary: Astronomers may have solved a cosmic mystery: strange, incredibly bright blue explosions called Luminous Fast Blue Optical Transients (LFBOTs) could be caused when black holes or neutron stars collide with the universe's hottest stars. A black hole is an object so dense that nothing, not even light, can escape its gravity, while a neutron star is the ultra-dense remnant of a dead star. These blue flashes are among the brightest explosions in space, but scientists have struggled to explain what causes them. If this theory is correct, it helps us understand extreme cosmic events and how these different objects interact. This discovery demonstrates how violent and dramatic the universe is, with collisions producing light so bright it can be detected from billions of miles away.

science-space.title: NASA's Psyche Mission Captures Mars During Gravity Assist Approach
science-space.summary: NASA's Psyche spacecraft has captured stunning images of Mars as it approaches the planet for a gravity assist maneuver scheduled for May 15, 2026. A gravity assist is when a spacecraft uses a planet's gravity to change direction and gain speed without using fuel—think of it like using Earth's gravity to slingshot a ball faster. The Psyche mission is traveling toward a metal-rich asteroid of the same name to study its composition and structure, which may help scientists understand how planets form. This Mars flyby is a crucial milestone on the two-year journey to reach the asteroid. The mission represents cutting-edge exploration technology and could reveal secrets about the building blocks of planetary formation, making it one of the most scientifically important missions in space exploration.

science-space.title: Blue Origin tests 1st moon lander ahead of lunar launch later this year
science-space.summary: Blue Origin, the space company founded by Amazon billionaire Jeff Bezos, is conducting rigorous tests on its first lunar lander spacecraft at NASA facilities across the nation. The lander is being prepared for its historic mission to the Moon, scheduled to launch later in 2026. These tests involve putting the spacecraft through simulated conditions it will encounter during landing, including extreme temperatures and challenging terrain. Blue Origin is competing with other private companies to become a major player in lunar exploration and potentially transport astronauts and cargo to the Moon. This represents a shift in space exploration: instead of only government agencies like NASA building spacecraft, private companies are now developing the technology. Success here could open doors for commercial Moon missions and make lunar exploration more accessible and frequent.

science-space.title: NASA Fuel Cell Tests Pave Way for Energy Storage on Moon
science-space.summary: NASA researchers are testing advanced fuel cell technology—devices that generate electricity through a chemical reaction between hydrogen and oxygen—to create a reliable energy storage system for lunar bases. The fuel cells being tested look like stacked metal cans and are monitored by nearly 270 sensors and 1,000 data collection points to track their performance under extreme conditions. This research is critical because the Moon has harsh environments with extreme temperatures and long periods of darkness, making reliable power essential for human missions. Fuel cells are particularly promising because they're efficient, produce water as a byproduct, and don't require constant charging like batteries. If successful, this technology could power future Moon bases and enable long-term human habitation, bringing us closer to establishing permanent settlements beyond Earth.

science-space.title: NASA's SpaceX 34th Commercial Resupply Mission Overview
science-space.summary: NASA and SpaceX are preparing to launch their 34th joint mission to resupply the International Space Station (ISS), targeting mid-May 2026. The SpaceX Dragon spacecraft, carried aboard the company's Falcon 9 rocket, will deliver approximately 6,500 pounds of critical supplies, scientific equipment, and investigations to the orbiting laboratory. This mission represents the ongoing partnership between NASA and private space company SpaceX, which has revolutionized how cargo reaches the Space Station. The Dragon spacecraft will autonomously dock—meaning it will attach itself without human control—to the ISS's forward port. These regular resupply missions are essential for keeping astronauts safe, healthy, and supplied with equipment for their scientific research, demonstrating how private companies are becoming integral to space exploration infrastructure.

science-space.title: NASA, Industry Advance High Performance Spaceflight Computing
science-space.summary: NASA and private aerospace companies are developing more powerful spacecraft computers that can operate reliably in the harsh radiation environment of space. These radiation-hardened processors—special computer chips designed to withstand intense radiation that would damage normal computers—have been the foundation of space exploration since the Apollo missions of the 1960s. Today's spacecraft need faster, more capable computers to handle complex navigation, communication, and scientific data analysis tasks. Traditional commercial computer processors, like those in your phone, don't work in space because radiation damages their circuits, so engineers must design specialized versions. This ongoing advancement is crucial because as missions become more ambitious—sending humans to the Moon and Mars—the computers controlling these spacecraft must become more sophisticated. Better space computers enable more advanced robotics, autonomous systems, and scientific instruments, making future deep-space exploration possible.

science-space.title: The charred hull of Artemis 2's Orion
science-space.summary: NASA's Artemis 2 Orion spacecraft has successfully completed its mission around the Moon and returned to Earth, with its hull charred and scorched from the intense heat of atmospheric reentry. The Artemis program is NASA's ambitious plan to return humans to the Moon for the first time since 1972, with Artemis 2 being a critical uncrewed test flight that paved the way for future crewed missions. The extreme heat damage visible on the spacecraft's exterior—reaching temperatures over 3,000 degrees Fahrenheit—is actually expected and confirms that the heat shield designed to protect astronauts is working as intended. This mission successfully tested all the major systems needed for human spaceflight, including launch, lunar orbit, and safe return to Earth. The successful completion of Artemis 2 represents a major milestone in human space exploration and brings us closer to establishing a sustainable presence on the Moon.

science-space.title: Pentagon unveils trove of declassified 'UFO' videos
science-space.summary: The Pentagon released a collection of previously classified UFO (Unidentified Flying Objects) videos on May 8, 2026, containing nearly 30 recordings featuring mysterious objects with unusual shapes and movements. These videos document encounters captured by military personnel and equipment, some showing objects described as football-shaped or as glowing white lights behaving in unexpected ways. The declassification represents increased government transparency about these unexplained sightings, though the videos don't prove the existence of aliens—UFO simply means the objects haven't been identified yet. Scientists and military officials are studying these videos to determine whether they show advanced foreign aircraft, natural phenomena, or something else entirely. This release is significant because it acknowledges that some aerial phenomena remain unexplained and deserve serious scientific investigation, encouraging researchers to study the evidence and develop better explanations for these mysterious encounters.

science-space.title: This is your best chance to spot the Milky Way's core in May
science-space.summary: May 2026 offers the perfect opportunity for stargazers to observe the glowing core of the Milky Way galaxy with their naked eyes, as it reaches its highest and most visible point in the night sky during spring. The Milky Way's core is the bright, cloudy band of light you see stretching across the sky—it's the center of our galaxy packed with billions of stars, creating an intense concentration of light visible even from Earth. This is possible because our solar system's position relative to the galactic center changes throughout the year, and May provides the ideal viewing angle. No special equipment is required; you just need to travel to a location with minimal light pollution (away from city lights) and look up at the right time. Observing the Milky Way connects us to our place in the universe and reminds us that we're part of a vast, ancient galaxy containing countless stars and potentially countless worlds.

finance-markets.title: Markets are banking on the 'Bliss trade'
finance-markets.summary: The stock market is currently soaring because investors believe governments will continue supporting their economies with massive spending and aid programs—a concept called the "Bliss trade." Think of it like this: if you knew your parents would always bail you out financially, you'd feel confident spending money freely. Right now, Wall Street is betting that central banks (government financial authorities that control money supply and interest rates) will keep interest rates low and provide economic support no matter what happens. This matters because it's keeping stock prices artificially high. If governments suddenly stop this support, or if people realize they won't continue helping, the entire stock market could crash. It's essentially a house of cards built on the assumption that state support will never end.

finance-markets.title: While AI pumps up portfolios, some companies sound the alarm
finance-markets.summary: While the stock market is booming thanks to artificial intelligence (AI) hype, many regular companies outside the "Big Tech" giants are actually in serious trouble, but nobody's noticing. Think of it like this: when everyone focuses on buying one hot stock, they ignore dozens of struggling businesses. The massive tech companies like Apple, Google, and Microsoft have gotten so huge and valuable that they're dominating stock market gains, making investors blind to the fact that smaller and medium-sized companies are struggling with debt, low profits, and weak demand. This creates a dangerous situation because if the AI bubble bursts or these tech giants stumble, there won't be healthy companies to cushion the fall. Essentially, the market's obsession with AI winners is masking a deeper problem in the broader economy.

finance-markets.title: Jane Street earned $10bn in first quarter as it doubled trading revenue
finance-markets.summary: Jane Street, a secretive but incredibly powerful trading firm on Wall Street, just made $10 billion in the first three months of the year—doubling the money it made from trading. To understand why this matters: Jane Street uses complex computer algorithms (mathematical instructions that execute trades automatically) to buy and sell financial assets at lightning-fast speeds, profiting from tiny price differences. They're one of the most profitable firms in finance, yet almost nobody's heard of them. Their massive profits show that the stock market is flooded with trading activity and volatility, which benefits companies that can process information fastest. This reveals how modern finance is dominated by artificial intelligence and speed rather than traditional stock picking, which means ordinary people investing normally have less of an advantage than ever before.

finance-markets.title: BlackRock Readies Launch of Two Tokenized Money-Market Funds
finance-markets.summary: BlackRock, the world's largest investment company managing over $10 trillion in assets, is creating new investment funds designed for people who hold money in "stablecoins"—digital currencies tied to regular dollars. To explain: a stablecoin is cryptocurrency that doesn't wildly fluctuate in value like Bitcoin; it stays roughly equal to one dollar. BlackRock's move signals that they believe digital dollars and cryptocurrency are here to stay as a mainstream way to store wealth. Money-market funds are investments that hold very safe, short-term debt (like government bonds) and pay interest. BlackRock launching these digital-focused funds means the financial establishment is finally accepting cryptocurrency as legitimate. For teenagers, this matters because it shows traditional finance and crypto are merging, which could make digital assets more accessible and trusted for everyday people in the future.

finance-markets.title: Pakistan to Receive $1.32 Billion as IMF Board Approves Tranches
finance-markets.summary: Pakistan just received approval from the International Monetary Fund (IMF—a global organization that lends money to countries in financial trouble) to receive $1.32 billion in emergency loans. Here's the background: Pakistan has been struggling with massive debt, a weak currency, and an economy on the brink of collapse. The IMF stepped in to provide a financial lifeline, but only if Pakistan agrees to strict reforms like cutting government spending and raising taxes. This loan matters because it prevents Pakistan's economy from crashing entirely, which would devastate its 240 million people and potentially trigger a global financial ripple effect. However, accepting IMF money comes with painful conditions—ordinary Pakistanis often face higher prices, fewer government services, and job losses. This story shows how fragile some developing economies are and how dependent they are on help from wealthy nations.

finance-markets.title: Ryan Cohen, the rebel CEO who disdains corporate America
finance-markets.summary: Ryan Cohen, the eccentric billionaire founder of Chewy (an online pet supply company), is attempting a $56 billion hostile takeover of eBay—meaning eBay's board doesn't want to sell, but Cohen is trying to force it anyway. Cohen is known for his rebellious style, cryptic social media posts, and disregard for traditional corporate rules. What makes this unusual is that Cohen appears to be using the same strategy as "meme stock" investors—everyday people coordinating on social media to buy stocks in companies they believe in, potentially driving up prices. A hostile bid means Cohen is appealing directly to eBay's shareholders (part-owners) rather than the company leadership, betting that shareholders will vote to accept his offer. If successful, this could be the first major corporate acquisition driven by grassroots internet enthusiasm rather than traditional finance logic. It exemplifies how power is shifting from corporate gatekeepers to retail investors organizing online.

finance-markets.title: Colombia Inflation Ticks Up, Fueling Case For Renewed Rate Hikes
finance-markets.summary: Colombia's inflation (the rate at which prices for everyday goods rise) increased in April, moving further away from the central bank's target of around 3%. When inflation rises, your money buys less stuff—if inflation is 10%, something that costs $100 today will cost $110 next year. Colombia's central bank had paused raising interest rates (the cost of borrowing money), hoping inflation would naturally cool down, but it's actually gotten worse. Now economists think the bank will need to resume hiking interest rates to combat inflation. Higher interest rates make borrowing money more expensive, which discourages people and businesses from spending and borrowing, theoretically cooling down inflation. However, higher rates also slow economic growth and make existing debts harder to repay. This matters globally because Colombia's inflation problem mirrors issues in many developing countries, showing how fragile their economies are to price pressures.

finance-markets.title: Honeywell-Backed Computing Firm Quantinuum Files for US IPO
finance-markets.summary: Quantinuum, a quantum computing company backed by industrial giant Honeywell, just filed paperwork to go public on the stock market—meaning regular people will soon be able to buy shares in it. Quantum computers are revolutionary machines that process information differently than regular computers, potentially solving problems that would take normal computers thousands of years. Quantinuum is capitalized because investors are extremely excited about quantum computing's future potential in areas like drug development, financial modeling, and artificial intelligence. When a company "goes public" through an IPO (initial public offering), it sells shares to raise money and becomes tradeable on stock exchanges. This matters because quantum computing could fundamentally transform industries within the next decade, and Quantinuum's IPO shows that venture capitalists believe the technology is moving from labs into real-world applications. However, IPO enthusiasm can be dangerously optimistic—companies overhype potential and stock prices can crash when reality disappoints.

finance-markets.title: Private Equity Taps Junk Market for Dividends as Exits Stall
finance-markets.summary: Private equity firms (investment companies that buy other companies to restructure and profit from them) are borrowing money through the "junk debt" market to pay themselves dividends because they're having trouble selling their investments for cash. Think of it this way: private equity firms usually make money by buying companies, improving them, then selling them at a higher price. However, market uncertainty from the Iran conflict and AI anxiety means fewer people want to buy companies right now, so exits are "stalled." Instead of waiting, these firms are taking on risky loans (called "junk" because they're issued by less creditworthy borrowers) to pay themselves money now. This is dangerous because it increases debt without generating actual profits, creating a house of cards vulnerable to collapse. It reveals how desperate some financial players are becoming and suggests the private equity boom might be losing steam as economic uncertainty grows.

finance-markets.title: Trump says Russia and Ukraine agree 3-day ceasefire and prisoner swap
finance-markets.summary: Donald Trump announced that Russia and Ukraine have agreed to a three-day ceasefire (temporary pause in fighting) and will exchange prisoners, though significant skepticism remains about whether Vladimir Putin (Russia's longtime leader) will actually commit to a lasting peace deal. Trump, who has positioned himself as a potential peace broker, claimed credit for negotiating this agreement, but geopolitical experts doubt it will hold. Russia and Ukraine have been at war since 2022, and previous ceasefire attempts have collapsed. A lasting peace deal is crucial because this war has disrupted global energy markets (causing oil price spikes), grain supplies (affecting food prices worldwide), and military spending. For teenagers, this matters because ongoing conflict keeps global economic uncertainty high, which affects stock markets, inflation, and job markets everywhere. Additionally, military spending diverts money governments could spend on education, healthcare, and infrastructure.

tech-ai.title: Cloudflare says AI made 1,100 jobs obsolete, even as revenue hit a record high
tech-ai.summary: Cloudflare, a major internet infrastructure company, just laid off 1,100 employees—its first large-scale layoff in company history. CEO Matthew Prince explained that artificial intelligence (AI) has become so efficient at handling customer support and other repetitive tasks that the company no longer needs as many workers in those roles. Think of AI as software that can learn and perform tasks humans normally do, often faster and cheaper. This matters because it signals a huge trend: as AI technology improves, many companies will likely eliminate jobs, even when they're making record profits. For teenagers entering the job market soon, this means certain types of work—especially routine, predictable tasks—might disappear before you graduate, forcing you to develop skills AI can't easily replace.

tech-ai.title: Poland says hackers breached water treatment plants, and the US is facing the same threat
tech-ai.summary: Poland's top intelligence agency revealed that Russian hackers have successfully broken into water treatment plants and other critical infrastructure—the systems that provide clean water to millions of people. This isn't just about stealing information; it's about sabotage, meaning attackers could potentially shut down these vital services or poison water supplies. The U.S. is reportedly facing identical threats. This matters enormously because water treatment is essential infrastructure—without it, cities can't function. Hackers (whether working for governments or alone) targeting these systems represents a terrifying new frontier in cyber warfare. If hackers can access water plants, they can also target hospitals, power grids, and other systems your community depends on, making cybersecurity a national security issue that affects your daily life.

tech-ai.title: DOGE used ChatGPT in a way that was both dumb and illegal, judge rules
tech-ai.summary: The Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) cancelled over $100 million in government grants using ChatGPT—an AI chatbot—to decide which grants to eliminate. The agency specifically targeted grants related to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI), which are programs designed to help underrepresented groups. A federal judge ruled this process unconstitutional because it violated proper legal procedures and free speech rights. ChatGPT is a language model AI that generates text responses, but it's not designed to make major legal decisions. This case matters because it's the first time a court has ruled on government misuse of AI, establishing an important precedent: even powerful AI tools can't replace careful human judgment in major decisions affecting people's lives and rights. It shows that AI is a tool that needs human oversight, especially in government.

tech-ai.title: Laid-off Oracle workers tried to negotiate better severance. Oracle said no.
tech-ai.summary: Oracle, one of the world's largest software companies, laid off numerous employees but refused to provide better severance packages (money given to workers when they're fired). The affected workers discovered they weren't protected by the WARN Act, a law requiring companies to give employees two months' notice before large layoffs, because Oracle had classified them as remote workers. Remote workers are people who work from home rather than at an office. This matters because it reveals a loophole: companies can potentially avoid legal protections by classifying workers in specific ways. For teenagers entering the workforce, this highlights why employment laws exist—to protect workers. It also shows that even at massive, successful companies, employees have limited power to negotiate better treatment during layoffs, making job security increasingly uncertain in the tech industry.

tech-ai.title: Uber partner Avride is under investigation for self-driving crashes
tech-ai.summary: Avride, a self-driving car company partnered with Uber, is under federal investigation after more than a dozen crashes and at least one injury involving its autonomous vehicles. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA)—the government agency that oversees car safety—opened this investigation to determine if Avride's self-driving technology is safe enough. Self-driving cars are vehicles controlled by artificial intelligence and computer systems instead of humans. This investigation is crucial because millions of people might soon use self-driving cars for transportation, but if the technology causes crashes, it could harm or kill people. This shows that while AI and self-driving technology are exciting innovations, they need rigorous government testing before they're widely available to the public. The outcome of this investigation could determine whether self-driving cars become mainstream transportation.

tech-ai.title: Apple reportedly has a deal to use Intel-made chips again
tech-ai.summary: Apple and Intel, two tech giants, have reportedly reached a preliminary agreement for Intel to manufacture computer chips for Apple devices. This is a big deal because Apple famously stopped using Intel chips years ago and switched to its own custom-designed chips called Apple Silicon, which work better and faster. A chip is the tiny computer processor that powers all devices—phones, laptops, everything. New Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan took over in March 2025, and the U.S. government recently invested 10 percent into Intel to keep the company competitive. This partnership matters because it signals that Intel is attempting a comeback and that Apple sees value in working with it again. For teenagers, this means the competition between tech companies drives innovation—when one company dominates (like Apple did), others push harder to improve. This deal could reshape the computer industry and lead to better, more competitive products.

tech-ai.title: US defense contractor who sold hacking tools to Russian broker ordered to pay $10M to former employers
tech-ai.summary: Peter Williams, a former cybersecurity executive at a U.S. defense contractor, stole surveillance and hacking tools from his employer and sold them to a Russian broker connected to Putin's government for $1.3 million. He's now been ordered to pay $10 million to his former employers. Cybersecurity tools are software that can break into computer systems—incredibly dangerous in the wrong hands. Williams betrayed national security by selling these tools to Russia, a geopolitical rival of the United States. This case highlights how individual employees can cause massive damage if they become traitors, and it shows why national security and cybersecurity are deeply connected. For teenagers, this demonstrates that technology can be weaponized in real geopolitical conflicts, and that people who work with sensitive technology have legal and ethical responsibilities to protect it, not sell it to hostile nations.

tech-ai.title: Here is Yarbo's promise to fix the robot mower that ran me over
tech-ai.summary: A reporter was actually run over by a hacked robot lawn mower made by Yarbo, a Chinese company, after a security researcher exposed thousands of these devices could be easily hijacked by hackers. When you hack something, you illegally access it and take control without permission. The hackers could steal personal information like GPS coordinates (your location), Wi-Fi passwords, and email addresses. Yarbo responded with a detailed plan to fix the security vulnerabilities (weak points in their system). This incident matters because it shows that as more physical devices become connected to the internet—from lawn mowers to cars to home security systems—they become targets for hackers. The fact that a teenager or casual hacker could control a device with spinning blades highlights the real-world dangers of poor cybersecurity. It proves that companies rushing to make "smart" devices must prioritize security, or people could literally get hurt.

tech-ai.title: Intel's comeback story is even wilder than it seems
tech-ai.summary: Intel's stock price has skyrocketed 490 percent over the past year—meaning if you'd invested $100 a year ago, it would be worth $590 today. Wall Street investors are betting heavily that Intel is making a major comeback as a company. However, analysts warn that this stock surge might be getting ahead of reality—the company might not actually be performing as well as the hype suggests. A stock is partial ownership in a company; when stock prices rise, investors profit. Intel had fallen behind competitors in recent years but is attempting a dramatic recovery. This matters because Intel is a foundational company in computing—most computers and servers use Intel chips. If Intel successfully rebounds, it affects the entire tech industry. For teenagers, this shows how investor confidence (or hype) can drive stock prices up even when the underlying business might not justify it, teaching an important lesson about how markets sometimes price things based on hope rather than reality.

tech-ai.title: All the latest updates on AI data centers
tech-ai.summary: Tech companies are rapidly building massive data centers—enormous warehouse-like facilities packed with thousands of powerful computers—to support artificial intelligence operations. These facilities require enormous amounts of electricity and are causing conflicts worldwide over environmental impact, power grid strain, rising utility bills, and effects on nearby communities. A data center is essentially a giant computer facility that processes and stores massive amounts of information for services you use online. AI requires exponentially more computing power than traditional software, making these data centers essential but environmentally costly. Some companies are proposing audacious ideas like launching data centers into space to avoid earthly problems. This matters because the infrastructure supporting AI innovation has real environmental and community consequences that affect your future. As AI becomes more powerful and prevalent, the physical infrastructure supporting it becomes increasingly important, and society must decide if the benefits of advanced AI justify the environmental costs and neighborhood disruptions.

energy-climate.title: DeBriefed 8 May 2026: EU eyes fossil-fuel exemptions | Wind and solar save UK ‘£1.7bn’ | Amazon ‘tipping point’
energy-climate.summary:
Welcome to Carbon Brief’s DeBriefed. An essential guide to the week’s key developments relating to climate...

energy-climate.title: Factcheck: What the UK car industry is not saying about EV targets
energy-climate.summary:
For several years, the UK car industry has been claiming that demand is not high...
The post Factcheck: What the UK car industry is not saying about EV targets appeared first on Carbon Brief.

Hong Kong schools have recorded net student rises for two consecutive years, with the current academic year seeing an increase of about 7,200 pupils, a surge that an industry leader has attributed to the influx of dependants of admitted talent.
The Education Bureau published enrolment figures on its website on April 28. The data covers all government, aided, private, international and direct subsidy scheme schools at the time of the annual headcount exercise in September 2025.

asia-pacific.title: China boy, 4, survives 11-floor fall after climbing onto window to see if parents were coming home
asia-pacific.summary:
A four-year-old boy miraculously survived after falling from the window of his 11th-floor home.
After the boy gained consciousness, he said in tears to his mother that he had climbed onto the window because he was missing her so much that he wanted to see if she was coming home.
The boy’s experience went viral online after his experience was reported by the Haibao News in early May.

asia-pacific.title: Mexico to end school year 40 days early for World Cup amid heat wave
asia-pacific.summary:
Mexican authorities have announced that the school year will end over a month early as the country gears up to host World Cup matches, prompting broad backlash.
Education Secretary Mario Delgado said the decision to end the school year 40 days early was made in part due to a heatwave affecting several states.
“We’re going to end the school year on June 5 because many states are already experiencing high temperatures, and there’s also the issue of the World Cup,” he said at an event on Friday in.

China has moved early to help Laos plan its longest highway, the latest project in Beijing’s push to expand transport connectivity in Southeast Asia.
The initiative underscores Beijing’s broader effort to strengthen its economic footprint in the region through roads and railway infrastructure, as it seeks alternative trade routes amid a fractious relationship with the United States.

asia-pacific.summary:
There is an “almost zero” chance that the Dutch man linked to the hantavirus outbreak on the MV Hondius cruise ship contracted the disease in the Argentine port of Ushuaia, a provincial health official said on Friday.
Juan Petrina, director of epidemiology for Tierra del Fuego province, where Ushuaia is located, told reporters that his assessment was based on the virus’s incubation period, among other factors.
